codeReview.summary: The implementation fully covers the feature requirements. ProviderTransport trait is object-safe (Send + Sync + 'static) via async_trait, with all required methods. ProviderEvent has 34 variants covering all 15 explicitly required event types plus 19 additional upstream Codex/ACP events and an Unknown catch-all. AgentType UniFFI enum has all 6 required variants with proper derives and Display impl. AgentInfo and SessionInfo UniFFI records have all required fields. DiscoveredServer and AppDiscoveredServer extended with agent_types: Vec<AgentType> defaulting to [Codex] for backward compatibility. 15 unit tests cover object safety, enum variants, serde round-trips, Display formatting, and agent_types defaults/conversions. Code quality is high — consistent with codebase patterns, well-documented with doc comments, and properly organized.

issues.description: Feature description says 'CommandDelta' but implementation uses 'CommandOutputDelta'. The more descriptive name is better and aligns with existing codebase naming (e.g. CommandExecutionData), but there's a minor mismatch with the feature spec.

Feature description says connect(config) -> Result<Self> but implementation uses connect(&mut self, config) -> Result<(), TransportError>. This is the correct design — returning Self from a trait method would break object safety. The implementation correctly uses &mut self since ProviderTransport must be object-safe.

event_receiver() method was not in the feature description but was added to the trait. This is a good addition for async streaming (next_event is non-blocking peek, event_receiver provides async streaming), but it does expose tokio::sync::broadcast::Receiver in the trait signature which couples the trait to tokio. This is acceptable since the crate already depends on tokio throughout.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker created .factory/library/provider-transport.md with comprehensive documentation of the provider transport layer including types, mapping pipeline, CodexProvider architecture, ServerSession integration, and error handling. This is a good practice that benefits future workers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: File at .factory/library/provider-transport.md with ~230 lines covering all provider types, architecture, and test coverage notes.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The ProviderEvent enum uses serde internally tagged representation (#[serde(tag = "type")]) which is consistent with JSON event patterns. The AGENTS.md mission file already documents the provider architecture pattern correctly, so no gap there.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: provider/mod.rs line ~145: #[serde(rename_all = "camelCase", tag = "type")] on ProviderEvent enum

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker followed the rust-backend-worker skill procedure correctly. skillFeedback.followedProcedure is true with no deviations.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff file shows skillFeedback.followedProcedure: true, deviations: []

summary: PASS. The provider-transport-trait feature is well-implemented and meets all validation assertions (VAL-PROV-001, VAL-PROV-007, VAL-PROV-010). The ProviderTransport trait is object-safe with Send + Sync bounds, AgentType has all 6 required UniFFI-safe variants, and DiscoveredServer carries agent_types with backward-compatible [Codex] default. ProviderEvent covers all 15 explicitly required event types plus 19 additional variants. Three minor non-blocking notes: CommandDelta was renamed to CommandOutputDelta (better name), connect returns () instead of Self (correct for object safety), and event_receiver() was added beyond spec (good addition). Test coverage is thorough with 15 new tests including compile-time object safety assertion.
